summary: Gruber amplifies former Apple AppKit engineer Peter Ammon's argument that the Mac app sandbox was a strategic mistake that weakens native Mac apps against the growing tide of web and Electron alternatives. Ammon argues that sandboxing undermines the Mac's core strengths — performance, system integration, and quality — while offering inferior security compared to web apps. Gruber agrees that code signing with revocable certificates has been a security win, but contends that sandboxing works against what makes the Mac distinct from iOS. He argues the Mac's entire purpose is to be a great platform for native apps, and that restricting their power pushes developers toward cross-platform alternatives. The real threat isn't screen-capture capabilities but the growing number of developers building non-native apps and users who don't mind using them.
key_points: The Mac app sandbox undermines the platform's core advantages over web and Electron apps: performance, system integration, and developer experience
Code signing with revocable certificates provides sufficient security for the Mac without the heavy-handed restrictions of sandboxing
Sandboxing works on iOS because it was designed in from the start, but the Mac was never built around it and suffers when it's imposed retroactively
If the Mac devolves into a platform for browsers and Electron apps, it loses its reason to exist as a distinct platform
The real problem facing the Mac isn't app security vulnerabilities but the proliferation of non-native apps and user indifference to quality
money_quotes: The whole point of the Mac is to be a great platform for native Mac apps.

If the Mac devolves into a platform where people just use web browsers and cross-platform Electron apps, it might as well not exist, because the only remaining thing that would distinguish it from other desktop OSes is iCloud integration.
It's the heaviness of the Mac that allows iOS to remain light.
The Mac needs the power for apps to shoot the user in the foot.
A Mac where all apps are guaranteed 'safe' is no longer a Mac.
The real problems facing the Mac are the number of developers creating non-native 'Mac' apps and the number of users who don't have a problem with them.
Further restricting sandboxed Mac apps would be solving a problem the platform doesn't have.
key_insight: The Mac's existential threat isn't security vulnerabilities but the erosion of native app culture, and sandboxing accelerates that erosion by handicapping the very apps that justify the platform's existence.
